title: Feeding Issues for Babies with CHD
text: B E A T F O R L E A D E R S H I P , S O W E G A H A S H E A R T , S U R V I V O R S H A V E H E A R T , # T h i s I s E M P O W E R , a n d t h e H e a r t - s h a p e d l o g o s a r e r e g i s t e r e d t r a d e m a r k s o f T h e M e n d e d H e a r t s , I n c . © 2 0 1 5 - 2 0 2 6 T h e M e n d e d H e a r t s , I n c . A l l R i g h t s R e s e r v e d ® ® ® ® ® ® ® ® T M T M T M T M T M T M T M Babies born with heart defects are more likely to have issues with feeding than babies without heart defects, especially babies who need surgery or medical intervention to survive. Some babies with congenital heart defects (CHDs) will have problems gaining weight and growing, and some will even have failure to thrive where their weight and height does not increase or declines. Feeding issues can affect a baby’s development during a hospital stay and even in childhood. Yet, many parents are unaware of feeding issues in babies with CHD, what questions to ask the healthcare team, and what they can do. Unfortunately, having a baby who has CHD is already stressful for parents, and being unprepared for feeding issues can increase that stress significantly. This publication will help parents understand feeding issues in babies with CHD, why they happen, and what they can do both during a hospital stay and when transitioning to home. When parents learn about their baby’s heart problem(s), either before birth or after, they often are focused on the heart problem(s) and not on other issues that might go along with heart problems, even when they are told about them at the same time. This makes sense as parents often go into “fight or flight” mode when faced with scary news or an upcoming surgery, and they can take in only limited information. Also, parents are typically most worried about their child’s survival and feel like they can deal with any other issues later. However, parents will often say that after the heart condition, feeding issues were the most unexpected and stressful issue they faced. Feeding Issues for Babies with CHD "My advice to ANY new mom is 'Do what feels right to YOU! You are the only one who knows what you can handle. And don't let anyone make you feel ashamed!'" Part of the Mended Little HeartGuide Series Page 1 When parents feed a new baby, they have important bonding time with that baby. For breastfeeding moms, there is skin-to-skin contact that allows the mom to feel close to the baby. Also, many moms feel one of the most important parts of their job as a mom is to provide nutrition for their baby. This is true for any mom—if something goes wrong with their ability to feed their baby, it is a major source of stress, even frustration and grief. They often feel they have failed. This stress affects mom and the baby, and often dad and the whole family as well. Understanding the causes of feeding issues and what can be done can reduce that stress. Babies with CHD are more likely to have feeding issues than babies without CHD, especially babies who require surgery to survive. There are many reasons for this. Blood flow and oxygenation. Babies with CHD may have blood flow issues and oxygenation issues that impact feeding. Low oxygen saturations (often called SATS) in the blood may make it difficult for babies to get enough oxygen when feeding. Blood flow to the lungs, or lack of blood flow to the lungs, may also affect a baby’s ability to breath and eat at the same time. Blood flow to the digestive tract may be reduced making it harder to digest food. Finally, having a heart that has to work extra hard to get blood to the body can make babies sleepier and less alert than babies without a CHD. Genetic syndromes. Almost one-third of babies with CHD have a genetic syndrome. Genetic syndromes may affect sucking and swallowing, alertness, coordination and other things that impact feeding. Medications. Babies with CHD are often given medications to help their heart function, to reduce pain, to reduce fluid build-up in their hearts and bodies, and for other reasons. Some of these medications affect the digestive system. These medications might also make the baby sleepy and therefore less likely to want

title: Feeding Issues for Babies with CHD
text: to work extra hard to get blood to the body can make babies sleepier and less alert than babies without a CHD. Genetic syndromes. Almost one-third of babies with CHD have a genetic syndrome. Genetic syndromes may affect sucking and swallowing, alertness, coordination and other things that impact feeding. Medications. Babies with CHD are often given medications to help their heart function, to reduce pain, to reduce fluid build-up in their hearts and bodies, and for other reasons. Some of these medications affect the digestive system. These medications might also make the baby sleepy and therefore less likely to want to feed. Interrupted or delayed feeding. Babies born with severe CHD may have their feeding interrupted or delayed due to the need for surgery or medical procedures. Some hospitals allow babies with CHD to be fed before surgery, but others do not allow oral feeding before surgery. Babies may not be able to be fed orally for several days, sometimes weeks or, in rare cases, months, after surgery, especially if the baby is on a respirator. This delay or interruption can cause problems with oral feeding, and a baby may have to relearn how to suck, swallow and breath after he or she recovers from surgery or a medical procedure. Respirators or oxygen. Babies with CHD may require oxygen either through a nasal cannula, respirator, or even extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). It can be difficult, if not impossible, to feed babies orally while receiving oxygen. What causes feeding issues? Page 2 Special diets/formulas. Some babies will develop complications that require special diets, such as low-fat or nonfat formulas, and/or fortification (adding calories to formula or breastmilk). Some of these formulas and fortifications cannot be given orally, and those that do must be given during bottle feeding. Lack of coordination. It is not uncommon for babies with CHD to require much more work with feeding or feeding through an NG tube or a G-tube after surgery because they are unable to coordinate sucking, swallowing and breathing well enough to get adequate nutrition. Babies with CHD also may have higher calorie needs than other babies because their hearts and their bodies are working extra hard. Also, babies with CHD may not be able to take in enough volume to get enough calories. "We were told by several doctors and nurses not to expect Kaleb to be able to nurse after surgery. He had a feeding tube in his nose immediately after his surgery. The first time he was able to wiggle around, he managed to pull it out. They replaced the tube and a few hours later he did the same thing again! Our nurse went to the doctor who gave us the okay to go ahead and try to nurse him. As soon as I held him in my arms, it was like he hadn’t ever stopped nursing." Feelings about feeding? covering from surgery. They have to deal with medications, formulas, feeding tubes and other equipment that their baby may need. Moms who wanted to breastfeed their baby may not be able to right away, or ever, and may have to supplement their child’s breastmilk with formula for extra calories even when they do breastfeed. Moms who bottle feed their baby may feel pressure to breastfeed and unsupported. These feelings are valid and expected, but what you feed your baby and how you feed your baby isn’t what’s most important. Your love for your baby is what matters the most. Babies who are breastfed, bottle fed, and tube fed who are loved, have the most important thing they need. Talk to your baby’s healthcare team about feeding your baby, ask questions, and you will make the decision that is best for your baby even if it wasn’t what you wanted or expected. Moms and dads of babies with CHD may have feelings of frustration, anger, grief and/or guilt about feeding their child. These feelings are completely normal. Before a baby is born, parents often have images of feeding their baby easily and peacefully. When a baby is born with CHD, this image can be shattered. Parents often have to learn how to feed their new baby while preparing for their baby’s surgery or medical procedure or while their baby is re- Page 3 droplets and lymph. Babies with chylous effusions may need chest tubes to drain the fluid, which can be painful or uncomfortable. They also are often put on a special nonfat or low-fat diet so the ducts can heal. This may impact feeding, particularly for breastfeeding babies. In addition to these reasons for issues with feeding, babies with CHD may develop other complications that impact feeding. Some of the main issues that affect feeding are: Necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC). Babies with CHD are at a higher risk of getting necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC). This is a disease that happens most often in premature infants where the wall of the intestine becomes infected with bacteria and inflamed. This is a very serious disease that can destroy the walls of the bowel and be fatal. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). Babies with CHD are

title: Feeding Issues for Babies with CHD
text: This may impact feeding, particularly for breastfeeding babies. In addition to these reasons for issues with feeding, babies with CHD may develop other complications that impact feeding. Some of the main issues that affect feeding are: Necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC). Babies with CHD are at a higher risk of getting necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC). This is a disease that happens most often in premature infants where the wall of the intestine becomes infected with bacteria and inflamed. This is a very serious disease that can destroy the walls of the bowel and be fatal. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). Babies with CHD are also more likely to get GERD, often referred to as reflux. Babies who have GERD have discomfort when feeding and right after feeding. These babies may start feeding and then stop and cry. This can be very difficult for the parent feeding the baby. This disease may be managed with medications, changes in the way the baby is fed and/or position while feeding. Chylous effusions. Babies with CHD can get chylous effusions after surgery when the lymph ducts that carry chyle to the body are injured. Chyle is a milky substance that contains fat droplets Complications that may affect feeding It is important for parents to understand why their baby may have difficulty feeding, but it is also important for parents to understand what and how their baby may be fed during a hospital stay. Total Parenteral Nutrition (TPN). TPN is when the nutrition the baby needs is given through a vein to bypass the digestive system. This can be very scary for parents because it is hard to understand how a baby can be adequately fed through an IV. Also, parents worry that their baby will not learn how to feed orally when they are on TPN for some time. Breastfeeding. Information and data around breastfeeding a baby with CHD is constantly changing. In the past, breastfeeding a baby with CHD, especially one who needed surgery or medical procedures, often was discouraged. In addition, breastfeeding was often not permitted before a baby’s first surgery if that surgery would be within the first weeks of life. Now, many hospitals are encouraging breastfeeding during the time before surgery and after surgery if it is safe to do so. Different ways babies receive nutrition in the hospital Today, there is more and more evidence that breastfeeding is beneficial for babies with CHD, even babies who need surgery. Where previously it was believed that too many calories were burned breastfeeding, babies may burn fewer calories breastfeeding. Also, it may be easier for babies to coordinate sucking, swallowing and breathing while breastfeeding. Despite this information, there are often still many mixed messages about breastfeeding a baby with CHD and communication can be unclear. Also, breastfeeding can be stressful even for moms who really want to breastfeed. Breastmilk production can be a concern (tips below), and moms may worry that their baby isn’t getting enough Page 4 Bottle feeding. For many reasons, including stress level of the mother, parents may choose to bottle feed their baby. The baby may require higher-calorie formula or fortified breastmilk that must be fed through a bottle or tube. Babies with CHD with chylous effusions might require nonfat or low-fat formulas as well. Some mothers pump their breastmilk to give to their baby or store until their baby can use it, and some will use formula. There are many high-quality formulas for babies, and the important thing is that the baby is getting adequate nutrition. breastmilk. Pumping breastmilk can seem like an added stressor as well, and it may be difficult to store pumped breastmilk. (See CDC guidelines for storing pumped breastmilk). Breastfeeding, especially exclusive breastfeeding, may not be an option for every baby with CHD. Some babies will need extra calories, and there is no way to fortify breastmilk without using another method of feeding like bottle feeding or tube feeding. Also, some babies with CHD will have difficulty feeding orally and require different methods of feeding. If the mother wanted to breastfeed but is unable to, bottle feeding formula can be very traumatic and make the mother feel inadequate. These are normal feelings, but mothers need to understand they are giving their baby the best chance to survive and to grow. Parents often find the NG tube stressful. Placing an NG tube can be difficult for parents who worry about hurting their child. NG tubes can accidentally be pulled out by the baby or parent and require reinsertion. Also, it can be difficult to find the right flow level on the pump or the right calorie level of the formula or milk, so the baby doesn’t vomit feeds. Finally, there is often not a clear plan for weaning from the NG tube. Nasogastric tube (NG tube). Some babies with CHD cannot get enough nutrition to survive and grow orally, and an NG-tube is inserted to help the baby get that nutrition. An NG tube is a hollow tube that is placed into the baby’s nostril, down the back of the throat, and into the stomach. Formula or breastmilk

title: Feeding Issues for Babies with CHD
text: Also, it can be difficult to find the right flow level on the pump or the right calorie level of the formula or milk, so the baby doesn’t vomit feeds. Finally, there is often not a clear plan for weaning from the NG tube. Nasogastric tube (NG tube). Some babies with CHD cannot get enough nutrition to survive and grow orally, and an NG-tube is inserted to help the baby get that nutrition. An NG tube is a hollow tube that is placed into the baby’s nostril, down the back of the throat, and into the stomach. Formula or breastmilk is pumped into the tube (often with a mechanical pump) and into the baby’s stomach. NG tubes are often used if the baby has chylous effusions so they can get special formula that can help heal the lymph ducts. NG tubes are also often used when the baby is having difficulty feeding orally. Some babies have trouble coordinating coordinating sucking, swallowing and breathing and need NG tube feeding until that happens. Also, babies with CHD often need more calories than babies without CHD to survive and grow, and NG tube feeding can allow more calories to be added to breastmilk or formula. Gastrostomy tube (G-tube). A G-tube is inserted into the baby’s belly to allow food to go directly into the stomach. This requires a surgical procedure and special formula. A G-tube is often used if the baby is going to need tube feeding for a longer time and for babies who cannot get adequate nutrition orally. Parents will need to learn how to care for a G-tube to keep it clean and prevent infections. The G-tube might be used to supplement feeding or to replace feeding altogether. Sometimes babies will need to get therapy to learn to feed orally after G-tube use. Caring for a G-tube video: https://youtu.be/yP3SYZVai18 Page 5 TPN. Babies who are getting TPN will get all of their nutrients through the IV. Again, it may feel like your baby isn’t getting adequate nutrition, but that is typically not the case. TPN is a temporary solution only used for babies who are too sick and/or on a respirator and can’t receive nutrition any other way. Breastmilk. Breastmilk from the mother or a donor typically provides the best nutrition for babies according to the American Academy of Pediatrics. Again, if the baby isn’t getting enough calories, breastmilk may need to be fortified. Many breastmilk fortifiers are made from cows’ milk. Today, there are human milk-derived fortifiers made from breastmilk donated by carefully screened mothers who are currently breastfeeding but have an excess that they wish to provide to other infants. Many hospitals have this available for your baby. At least one human milk-derived fortifier has been shown to reduce incidence of NEC and other complications in premature babies and is now being studied in babies with CHD. Formula. There are many types of formula available for babies with CHD. Parents should discuss what formula is best for their baby and why with their baby’s healthcare team. Types of food for babies Page 6 Some formulas have no fat or are low-fat for babies who have chylous effusions. Babies with chylous effusions may need these formulas to heal the lymph ducts. There are some nonfat formulas that can only be given through an NG tube or G tube and not orally. These are often given in the hospital only but are changed to a different formula once the baby goes home. Babies with CHD may have GERD and other feeding sensitivities that impact that formula that is chosen. Again, this should be clearly communicated to the parents so the parents can make an informed decision about which one is best. Sometimes, a baby doesn’t do well with one formula and will need to change to another. It can be trial and error to find the best one for your baby. "I was prepared to breast feed and/or bottle feed my son after birth. Nobody prepared me to watch my son eat via IV for his first 19 days. Then they gave him an NG tube, and that was how he received my precious breastmilk fortified with formula. No suckling, no bottle ever, and nothing normal! However, I was relieved that he lived through each day." Pump or breastfeed frequently, every 2-3 hours, if possible. If your baby is nursing but tires easily, try to pump after breastfeeding. This can be very exhausting, but it is important to keep the supply up. Drink lots of water and/or hydrating fluids. Make sure you stay hydrated. Drinking water can help you feel better in other ways too. Ask if you can pump in the room with your baby rather than in a pumping room or by yourself. Looking at your baby can help with milk supply. “They directed me to the “lactation room”- an empty room with pump and chair and nothing else. What helped me more than anything was when I started pumping in her room while looking at her, even though I couldn’t hold her! Made all the difference!” Eat

title: Feeding Issues for Babies with CHD
text: supply up. Drink lots of water and/or hydrating fluids. Make sure you stay hydrated. Drinking water can help you feel better in other ways too. Ask if you can pump in the room with your baby rather than in a pumping room or by yourself. Looking at your baby can help with milk supply. “They directed me to the “lactation room”- an empty room with pump and chair and nothing else. What helped me more than anything was when I started pumping in her room while looking at her, even though I couldn’t hold her! Made all the difference!” Eat nutritious meals. Skipping meals or eating unhealthy meals can affect overall health and milk supply. This can be difficult during a hospital stay. The hospital’s social worker or Child Life Specialist may have advice. Some hospitals provide a free meal for breastfeeding moms, so be sure to get your meal if that is a service the hospital offers. Find ways to relax. Spend some time each day just breathing in a quiet space, meditating, taking a walk, or doing something to help you relax. Stress can affect breast milk supply, so finding ways to manage stress will help. Use warm compresses. Some moms find that using warm packs on their breasts before pumping can help with supply. Talk to a lactation consultant. Many hospitals have a lactation consultant who will work with you and help you breastfeed your baby. Use the right pump. If you are pumping breastmilk, be sure you have the right pump. Some pumps are very difficult to use and take a long time to pump enough breast milk. Talk to the hospital social worker or Child Life Specialist to see if the hospital has pumps available for a discount or at no cost. Make sure you are moving. Movement, such as walking, can help with breastmilk production. Always ask your child’s medical team before eating, drinking or taking anything to enhance breastmilk production. Some substances can interfere with medication effectiveness or TIPS FOR BREASTMILK PRODUCTION Moms in the hospital with a new baby can have difficulty producing enough breastmilk. This is often because mom is tired, not drinking enough water, and has a very high stress level. This is normal, but there are things moms can do that may help. Here are some of them. Page 7 Can I breastfeed my baby before surgery? Can I pump and store breast milk while my baby is in surgery and recovering? How soon after surgery will my baby be able to breastfeed? What milestones must my baby meet before I can start or resume breastfeeding after surgery? What support and services does your hospital have for breastfeeding moms? How will I know if my baby is getting enough nutrition? BREASTFEEDING Why does my baby need a feeding tube? What is the difference between the types of feeding tubes? Can I breastfeed or bottle feed my baby while he or she is on a feeding tube? Can I give my baby breastmilk through the feeding tube? How can my baby learn to feed orally while on a feeding tube? What if my baby pulls out the feeding tube? What is the best speed for the feeding pump for my baby? Are there services available to help with the feeding tube once we are home? What is the plan for getting my baby off the feeding tube? FEEDING TUBES Q U E S T I O N S F O R Y O U R B A B Y ' S H E A L T H C A R E T E A M What type of formula is best for my baby and why? What are my options for formulas? How can I get the formula my baby needs once we are home? Are there discount programs or ways we can get formula at no cost? How can I keep track of the amount of formula my baby is getting? FORMULA Why does my baby need a fortifier? What are the types of fortifiers available and what are the ingredients in each type? What are the advantages and disadvantages of each type of fortifier? How long will my baby be on a fortifier? If I am breastfeeding, how will my baby get fortified breastmilk? FORTIFIERS Often, parents do not think they have choices when it comes to feeding their baby, but they do. The nutrition your baby gets is extremely important, and parents should be part of the decision-making when it comes to what and how their baby is fed. Again, often the focus is on the baby’s heart, but feeding affects the heart and the whole body. Don’t be afraid to ask questions when it comes to feeding your baby. Here are just some questions you might ask your baby’s healthcare team. Heading home with a baby who has CHD and has been in the hospital can be very scary and feeding issues can make it even more difficult. When the baby is in the hospital, they are often on a schedule for feeding and nurses are

title: Feeding Issues for Babies with CHD
text: decision-making when it comes to what and how their baby is fed. Again, often the focus is on the baby’s heart, but feeding affects the heart and the whole body. Don’t be afraid to ask questions when it comes to feeding your baby. Here are just some questions you might ask your baby’s healthcare team. Heading home with a baby who has CHD and has been in the hospital can be very scary and feeding issues can make it even more difficult. When the baby is in the hospital, they are often on a schedule for feeding and nurses are keeping track of wet diapers and making sure the baby is getting enough nutrition. Also, in the hospital parents usually have support, and that includes support for feeding their baby. Some babies will come home from the hospital with an NG tube or G-tube and all that entails. In addition, many babies will come home on oral medications that they must take on a schedule. All of this can be overwhelming and hard to manage. There are things parents can do to make the transition home easier. First, parents will want to be sure they have all the information they need, including who to call about feeding, before heading home. Make sure that is written or stored electronically in a place that is easy to get to. Also, parents will want to be sure they have all of the equipment they need and enough formula to get through a few days before arriving at home. No parent wants their baby to miss a meal because they don’t have the food or equipment. When you get home, find out where you can get the formula your baby needs, if any, and how much it costs. Some formulas can be harder to find than others. Once home, use the forms in the Mended Little HeartGuide to keep track of feeding and growth. Your child’s healthcare team may send you home with a scale to keep track of your baby’s weight as well. Remember to look at progress with weigh rather than comparing each day to the next. Baby’s rather than comparing each day to the next. Baby’s weight can fluctuate, but an upward trend is what you are looking for. Find out from your child’s doctor how much weight your baby should be gaining. If you don’t have a scale at home, be sure to schedule regular appointments with your baby’s pediatrician. Make sure your baby is having 6-8 wet diapers a day to ensure he or she is getting enough breastmilk or formula. Do not give your baby water, however, because water doesn’t have any calories that your baby needs. Be sure not to put medications in your baby’s formula. Give them separately so you are sure your child gets all the medication. If your baby is having a problem taking a medication, call your baby’s doctor right away to find a solution. Follow all instructions with medications. You can keep track of medications using the Medication Tracking Form in the Mended Little HeartGuide. Heading home from the hospital "When coming home from the hospital, we wrapped our heads around our son needing support via a feeding tube and reminded ourselves, 'This is ok. He will get the rest and nourishment he needs to grow stronger.' As parents, we leap over obstacles and tackle situations we never dreamed we could. We do it because we are parents. We are advocates. And, because we would move mountains for our kids." Page 9 E D U C A T E Y O U R S E L F Knowledge is power. The more you can learn about your baby’s CHD and any feeding and developmental issues that might come with it, the better prepared you will be and the more confident you will feel when making feeding decisions. M A N A G E S T R E S S Take care of yourself and find ways to manage stress. Feeding itself can sometimes be stressful, so if you find ways to manage stress at other times, feeding will go better. If you can, try to get enough sleep and eat well. Take walks and exercise. This may seem impossible, but even if you can find small ways to manage stress and calm yourself, you will feel better overall. F I N D A Q U I E T P L A C E Find a quiet, peaceful place, if possible, to feed your baby. Often this is almost impossible in the hospital with all the noise and light, but some hospitals now have quiet times. This is a perfect time to feed your baby. At home, try to find a place to make feeding more enjoyable for the person feeding the baby and for the baby too. This can help set a positive tone for feeding. Feeding is a time to be with your baby, and it is important to make it as pleasant as possible. F O L L O W Y O U R I N S T I N your baby. Often this is almost impossible in the hospital with all the noise and light, but some hospitals now have quiet times. This is a perfect time to feed your baby. At home, try to find a place to make feeding more enjoyable for the person feeding the baby and for the baby too. This can help set a positive tone for feeding. Feeding is a time to be with your baby, and it is important to make it as pleasant as possible. F O L L O W Y O U R I N S T I N C T If you feel like something isn’t right with your baby, talk to your baby’s healthcare team right away. Worst-case scenario, nothing is wrong, but it’s far better to be wrong than to be right and not tell anyone. S E E K S U P P O R T Feeding a baby with CHD can be very emotional, and parents can feel scared and overwhelmed. Get a support system. Family and friends can be helpful. There are any support groups of parents who have been through similar experiences and can provide support from someone who has been there. T I P S F R O M P A R E N T S M A N A G I N G F E E D I N G I S S U E S I N T H E H O S P I T A L A N D A T H O M E B E H A P P Y W I T H P R O G R E S S ! Don’t measure success as a comparison with other babies your child’s age. It’s easy to get caught up in a tangle of what your child “should be able to do”. Don’t dwell on this!! Focus on how your baby is improving and the positive aspects you have been able to achieve with your child’s care. This is what is most important. Finally, if you feel frustrated, overwhelmed or are unable to feed your baby, call your child’s doctor so he or she can help you find support. Sometimes, babies will need additional help feeding at a feeding clinic or with other specialists and getting that help as early as possible is important. Page 10 Little hearts hold big hopes. The Mended Hearts, Inc. (MHI) is the largest cardiovascular peer-to-peer support organization in the United States and has been providing hope and support to patients and families for more than 75 years! MHI’s programs—Mended Little Hearts®, Young Mended Hearts®, and Mended Hearts®— make MHI the only organization in the nation that supports patients with all forms of cardiovascular disease throughout the entire lifespan of the patient. MHI volunteers inspire, educate, and empower more than 380,000 patients and family members each year through a robust network of more than 96,000 members who provide services and outreach in hospitals, cardiology offices, clinics, cardiac rehab centers and local communities. MHI members don’t simply tell patients and families about the cardiovascular disease journey, they show them and walk with them on that journey. When you need us, we are there--you are not alone! www.mendedlittlehearts.org

title: Feeding Issues -WH CHD
text: F E E D I N G Y O U R B A B Y F O L L O W Y O U R I N S T I N C T If you feel like something isn’t right with your baby, talk to your child’s health care team right away. Worst-case scenario, nothing is wrong, but it’s far better to be wrong than to be right and not get help because you were worried about feeling embarrassed. M A N A G E S T R E S S It can be very difficult not to feel stress about feeding your baby. Parents often worry about things like whether their baby is getting enough calories or having difficulty feeding. While getting enough calories is important, so is your stress level. Take care of yourself and find ways to manage stress. If you can, try to get enough sleep, eat well, take walks outside and exercise. This may seem impossible, but even if you can find small ways to manage stress, you will feel better and you may find feeding less stressful too. F I N D A Q U I E T P L A C E Find a quiet, peaceful place, if possible, to feed your baby. This may have been difficult in the hospital with all the noise and light, but when you get home, try to find a calm place to make feeding more enjoyable. This can help set a positive tone for feeding and reduce stress. Even if your baby has a feeding tube, you can still hold your baby in a quiet place. Feeding is a time to bond with your baby, and it is important to make it as pleasant as possible. G E T S U P P O R T Feeding a baby with CHD can be very emotional, and parents can feel scared and overwhelmed. Build a support network. Start with health care providers who can help you with feeding issues. Ask your child's doctor and nurses if you don't know where to start. Family and friends can be helpful too. Also, there are many support groups of parents who have been through similar experiences that can provide support from someone who has been there. E D U C A T E Y O U R S E L F Knowledge is power. The more you can learn about your baby’s CHD and any feeding and developmental issues that might come with it, the better prepared you will be and the more confident you will feel when making feeding decisions. If possible, before you leave the hospital, ask your child's nurses and doctor about educational resources. Bringing a child home from the hospital can feel scary, and feeding can feel stressful. Get help if you need it. You can find more information and helpful tips on feeding your baby at www.mendedhearts.org. Parents often say that after worrying about their child’s health, feeding their baby is the most stressful thing they deal with when coming home from the hospital. Babies born with heart conditions, especially those who had surgery, may have feeding issues and will need extra support. This is normal. Don't be afraid to get the help you need. Here are some helpful tips from parents: B E H A P P Y W I T H P R O G R E S S Don’t measure success by comparing your baby with other babies your child’s age. It’s easy to get caught up in a tangle of what your child “should be able to do.” Focus instead on how your baby is improving and the positive things you have been able to achieve with your child’s care. Every baby will progress in their own time. If you have questions about your baby's development, contact your child's health care team. T O P A R E N T S F R O M P A R E N T S M e n d e d H e a r t s ® , M e n d e d L i t t l e H e a r t s ® , Y o u n g M e n d e d H e a r t s ® , H e a r t G u i d e ® , M y H e a r t V i s i t ® , A C T I O N N E T W O R K ™, R o c k Y o u r S c a r ® , S h a r e Y o u r H e a r t ® , E m p o w e r ™, E m p o w e r H F ™, E m p o w e r C H D ™, E m p o w e r C K D ™, E m p o w e r H e a r t A t t a c k ™, E m p o w e r O R K ™, R o c k Y o u r S c a r ® , S h a r e Y o u r H e a r t ® , E m p o w e r ™, E m p o w e r H F ™, E m p o w e r C H D ™, E m p o w e r C K D ™, E m p o w e r H e a r t A t t a c k ™, E m p o w e r S t r o k e ™, E m p o w e r T r a n s p l a n t ™, a n d t h e H e a r t - s h a p e d l o g o s a r e r e g i s t e r e d t r a d e m a r k s o f T h e M e n d e d H e a r t s , I n c . © 2 0 2 0 - 2 0 2 6 T h e M e n d e d H e a r t s , I n c . title: Neurodevelopmental Issues - WH CHD
text: M A N A G I N G D E V E L O P M E N T A L I S S U E S K N O W Y O U R T E A M It's important to understand what specialists work with neurodevelopmental issues and how to get support and services your child needs. Sometimes, your child’s pediatrician and cardiologist can help connect you with early intervention services for your child like physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and feeding therapy. In some areas, there are developmental pediatricians and/or neurodevelopmental programs for children born with CHD. Don’t be afraid to contact a program in another location if there isn’t one near you. G E T Y O U R C H I L D S C R E E N E D Your child’s cardiologist should be familiar with recommended screening for neurodevelopmental issues and at what ages your child should be screened. The American Heart Association has screening guidelines that you can review. As of 2022, screening is recommended at 9, 18 and 30 months and again before starting school at about age 4. These guidelines may be updated. If your child has a genetic condition or signs of more severe developmental issues, additional screenings may be needed. A V O I D C O M P A R I N G A N D C E L E B R A T E S U C C E S S E S When your child isn’t meeting milestones at the same time as other children or has some behavioral issues, it can be hard not to feel frustrated and discouraged. Avoid comparing your child to other children and focus on your child’s progress instead. With treatment, your child is likely to progress. It can be helpful to keep a journal and look back and see how far your child has come. Be sure to celebrate even the small wins along the way. G E T S U P P O R T Dealing with developmental, learning and behavioral issues can be difficult and emotionally challenging for parents. It can help to put a support system in place. Your child's health care team can be helpful in providing support and recommending support resources. You may have resources in your local area or state. Also, there are support groups for parents who have been through similar experiences, and they can provide support from someone who has been there. E D U C A T E Y O U R S E L F It is always helpful to understand your child’s heart condition and some of the issues that can go along with it like neurodevelopmental issues. When you educate yourself, you are better able to recognize when there is a problem or delay, and you can get the help and support you and your child need. You are also able to make decisions that can help your child have a better chance at success. It can be hard to deal with developmental issues and CHD at the same time. You can find more information and helpful tips on dealing with developmental issues at www.mendedlittlehearts.org. Children born with congenital heart defects (CHDs) who need surgery or medical intervention often have what are called “neurodevelopmental” issues. This term can be scary for parents, but it simply means that your child may not meet developmental milestones at the same time as expected for children their age and may be more likely to have autism spectrum disorders. Getting help from specialists can make a big difference for you and your child. Here are some tips from parents too: T O P A R E N T S F R O M P A R E N T S Mended Hearts , Mended Little Hearts , Young Mended Hearts , HeartGuide , MyHeartVisit , ACTION NETWORK , Rock Your Scar , ShareYourHeart , EmpowerHF , EmpowerCHD , EmpowerCKD , EmpowerHeartAttack , EmpowerStroke , EmpowerTransplant , EmpowerHypertension , and Survivors Have Heart , and the Heart-shaped logos ® ® ® ® ® ® ® ® TM TM TM TM TM TM TM TM

title: Preparing for a doctor appointment - WH CHD
text: P R E P A R I N G F O R A H E A L T H C A R E A P P O I N T M E N T T A K E A N O T H E R A D U L T W I T H Y O U , I F P O S S I B L E Two sets of ears are often better than one. It can be hard to remember everything the doctor said, so having another adult listener can help with that. Be sure it is someone who will be supportive. ? H A V E Q U E S T I O N S A N D C O N C E R N S R E A D Y Make a list of questions for your child’s doctor including any concerns you have. Most parents question what is “normal” and what isn’t. If you have a partner or spouse, ask for their questions too. When at an appointment, it can be easy to forget what you wanted to ask, so having a list prepared will help. Don’t be afraid to write the answers too. K N O W W H A T I S E X P E C T E D A N D W H A T T E S T S A R E I N V O L V E D Find out what tests your child will have and what is expected in the appointment before you go. Some tests will involve your child not eating for a period of time, so make sure you follow instructions if your child will need to stop eating and drinking before the appointment. T A K E W A T E R A N D A S N A C K Staying hydrated is important for you and your child. Be sure to bring water (breastmilk or formula for a baby). Also, if you can, pack a snack for you and your child in case you need it. (Be sure to check with a nurse before feeding your child before an appointment or test.) T A K E M E D I C A T I O N S A N D E Q U I P M E N T W I T H Y O U Take medication and equipment with you to appointments. Sometimes appointments go longer than expected or a dose of medication is due during the appointment. Take any medical equipment your child needs. Some non-medical equipment like strollers can help you if your child needs a test in a different location and with coming and going. Things that can sooth or amuse your child like favorite toys can be very helpful during appointments too. Preparing for your child's health care appointment can be confusing and overwhelming. You can find more information and helpful tips for preparing yourself and your child for health care appointments at www.mendedlittlehearts.org. Before you left the hospital with your child, you likely set up follow up appointments. Be sure to keep those appointments. Taking your baby or child to their first appointment after hospitalization can feel hectic, especially if you have a new baby. Here are some tips from parents to help you prepare: P R E P A R E Y O U R C H I L D A child life specialist can help you prepare your child for a health care appointment. If there is no child life specialist available, you can ask a nurse if there are any resources to help you prepare your child for an upcoming appointment. Ask other parents who have been through similar situations. There are also children’s books that can help you prepare your child. F I L L O U T F O R M S A N D C H E C K I N S U R A N C E C O V E R A G E Often, you can fill out medical history and other forms before your appointment. This will save you time and allow you to find answers you need to some questions. Also, check your insurance coverage (if applicable) and make sure you have completed all requirements for coverage. T O P A R E N T S F R O M P A R E N T S Mended Hearts , Mended Little Hearts , Young Mended Hearts , HeartGuide , MyHeartVisit , ACTION NETWORK , Rock Your Scar , ShareYourHeart , EmpowerHF , EmpowerCHD , EmpowerCKD , EmpowerHeartAttack , EmpowerStroke , EmpowerTransplant , EmpowerHypertension , and Survivors Have Heart , and the Heart-shaped logos ® ® ® ® ® ® ® ® TM TM TM TM TM TM TM TM

title: Transitioning from Hospital to Home -WH CHD
text: T R A N S I T I O N I N G F R O M H O S P I T A L T O H O M E L E A R N A B O U T Y O U R C H I L D ' S M E D I C A T I O N If your child needs to take medication, find out what it does, how it works, what side effects may occur, how much he or she needs and when it should be given. Plan ahead and ask for prescriptions to be arranged and possibly filled before your child is discharged. Some prescriptions need to be compounded (made into liquid), and not every pharmacy is able to do this. Get help to find out which pharmacies can manage your child’s medications. When you get home, the pharmacist will explain how to store the medications properly and help you understand medication instructions. Don't be afraid to ask questions. U N D E R S T A N D Y O U R C H I L D ' S E Q U I P M E N T Your child may be discharged with no equipment other than a pacifier, or you may be sent home with an order for home health to provide you with equipment for your child. Make sure you understand what equipment you will be getting, what it is for and how to use it. If possible, get information in writing or have it emailed to you before you leave the hospital. Also make sure you have information about who to call if you have questions about the equipment or if it isn’t working properly. K N O W W H A T T O D O I N A N E M E R G E N C Y Plan for the worst but expect the best. Start by learning cardio pulmonary resuscitation (CPR). As the parent or caregiver of a child with CHD, you’ll probably be taught how to do CPR at the hospital. If you have not been taught before discharge, ask. Also, learn when you should call 911 or your child’s doctor, and make an emergency plan. Go over that plan with your child’s health care providers before you leave the hospital. L E A R N F R O M N U R S E S In the hospital, nurses are at the bedside doing everything from changing diapers to providing life-saving care. When you get home, you’ll take over some of the care they provide in the hospital, so don’t be afraid to ask them to teach you things you need to learn. Nurses are a wonderful resource and are there to support you as you learn to care for your child as he or she gets ready to go home. E D U C A T E Y O U R S E L F A B O U T Y O U R C H I L D ' S C O N D I T I O N Before being discharged from the hospital, ask as many questions as necessary to get a solid understanding of your child’s heart condition and any surgeries or procedures your child had and may need in the future. Learning about your child’s condition will help you feel more confident and more in control when making decisions and managing your child's care. Ask your child’s health care provider for a list of reliable resources you can use to learn more. The reality of bringing your child home from a hospital stay can be both exciting and terrifying. While your child was in the hospital, doctors and nurses were his or her primary caregivers. Once you’ve returned home, you are faced with taking over care, which can be very scary. Here are some things you can do to help you feel more at ease: Bringing a child home from the hospital can feel scary. You can find more information and helpful tips for transitioning from hospital to home at www.mendedlittlehearts.org.
